Intro1: Reading comprehension is an incredibly important life skill, and one that is becoming increasingly vulnerable in the age of AI. Truly understanding a text is about much more than summarising the key points. Reading well (and writing well) involves a spectrum of skills. This includes making sense of ideas, drawing out subtlety, following arguments, and noticing when things don’t quite add up (Kintsch, 1998; Perfetti & Stafura, 2014).
Intro2: When machines do more of the interpreting we spend less time practicing the effortful work of deep reading. Over time, this is likely to weaken our ability to follow complex texts or retain what we’ve read. This is especially concerning given the deep links between reading and thinking. A diminished ability to understand complex ideas in written form may translate into a reduced ability to grapple with complex ideas in general. The good news is that comprehension is highly trainable. Research shows that active practices such as answering targeted questions and reflecting on meaning can significantly improve understanding (National Reading Panel, 2000; McNamara, 2007).
Intro3: The exercises below are not designed to trick you, and they should not feel excessively difficult for someone who actively engages their mind. The goal is less about finding a single “correct” answer and more about encouraging careful mental involvement. The questions usually contain some subtle message beyond the immediate literal one which requires some engaged interaction with the text to recognize.
Ref_Kintsch: Kintsch, W. (1998). Comprehension: A paradigm for cognition. Cambridge University Press.
Ref_McNamara: McNamara, D. S. (2007). Reading comprehension strategies: Theories, interventions, and technologies. Lawrence Erlbaum Associates.
Ref_NRP: National Reading Panel. (2000). Teaching children to read: An evidence-based assessment of the scientific research literature on reading and its implications for reading instruction. National Institute of Child Health and Human Development.
Ref_Perfetti: Perfetti, C. A., & Stafura, J. (2014). Word knowledge in a theory of reading comprehension. Scientific Studies of Reading, 18(1), 22–37.
